title: Quantifying Exposure Bias for Open-ended Language Generation

structured.summary: The paper investigates the exposure bias issue in maximum likelihood estimation training of language models, introducing metrics EB-M and EB-C to measure its impact. Results suggest exposure bias may have a minimal effect on performance degradation, as models showed self-recovery abilities despite prefix distortions.

strengths.point: Introduction of new metrics to assess exposure bias.
strengths.evidence: The paper proposes EB-M and EB-C to quantify the effects of exposure bias on quality, diversity, and consistency in language generation.
Results indicate minimal performance loss from training-generation discrepancies, questioning the severity of exposure bias.

weaknesses.evidence: The experiments focus primarily on the wiki-103 dataset, limiting generalizability across diverse language modeling tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of clarity in metric definition.
weaknesses.evidence: Descriptions of EB-M and EB-C lack depth, making it difficult to fully understand their scope and application.
Hyperparameters, model configurations, and data processing steps are inadequately detailed, hindering replication efforts.

questions.question: How do EB-M and EB-C metrics compare to existing metrics?
questions.motivation: Understanding the novelty and added value of the proposed metrics in the context of well-established evaluation methods.

critique_points.point_001: Limited dataset use.. The experiments focus primarily on the wiki-103 dataset, limiting generalizability across diverse language modeling tasks
critique_points.point_002: Lack of clarity in metric definition.. Descriptions of EB-M and EB-C lack depth, making it difficult to fully understand their scope and application
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient experimental reproducibility details.. Hyperparameters, model configurations, and data processing steps are inadequately detailed, hindering replication efforts
